songs.scenario: the fight that ended it with a teacher who believed in me, in a town that's barely there anymore
songs.story:
The town used to have a name people recognized. Now it’s just a bend in the coastal road where the sign is rusted through and the gulls outnumber the roofs.

I remember the classroom most clearly in winter. The radiators clanged like someone knocking from inside the pipes. Frost crawled up the inside of the windows, and Mr. Halberg would still insist we keep them cracked open “for thinking air.” He believed in me in a way that made me straighten my back without noticing. He handed me books like they were tools, not gifts—dog-eared copies of essays, maps with pencil marks in the margins, a battered anthology that smelled faintly of tobacco and old glue.

I wasn’t used to being seen like that.

It started unraveling the day the paper came back. My essay about the harbor closure—about how the cranes stood like broken necks over empty water—had red ink all through it. Not corrections. Questions. Too many of them. One in the margin: Why do you keep writing like everything is already lost?

After class I stayed behind. The hallway smelled of wet wool coats and chalk dust. I told him he didn’t understand what it was like to stay in a place that was disappearing while you were still inside it. He said understanding wasn’t the point. Then he said something I still hear in pieces: that I was choosing to write from the end of things instead of the middle where people could still reach me.

I laughed. It came out sharp, wrong. I said he liked the idea of me more than me. That I was just a project to keep him from feeling useless in a dying town.

His face changed—not anger, not yet. Something like disappointment landing too hard.

We were outside before I realized I was walking. The wind off the fjord cut through my jacket, carrying salt and diesel from the last working ferry. The school bell rang even though no one was inside anymore; it had been set on a timer years ago and never corrected.

He told me I was wrong about him. I told him he was wrong about everything else. The words escalated without permission, as if the empty buildings around us were echoing them back louder each time.

The turning point wasn’t a shout. It was when he said, very quietly, “I believed you would stay open.”

That sentence did something worse than anger. It made me realize he had been holding a version of me I couldn’t live up to. I told him I was done being shaped by someone else’s hope.

He didn’t stop me when I walked away down the cracked road past the shuttered bakery and the bus stop with no timetable. That was the last lesson, though neither of us meant it to be.
songs.lyrics:
There’s a town that forgot its own name
Where the map just gives up at the coast
Gulls circle the rooftops like memories
And the wind is the only one home
The sign’s rusted through like a secret
Half letters, half weathered regret
And the sea keeps on taking its distance
Like it’s something it hasn’t finished yet

I remember the winter classroom
Radiators knocking like bone
Frost writing its name on the windows
While we tried to pretend we were warm
Mr. Halberg said, “keep it cracked open”
“For thinking, for air, for the mind”
And he handed me books like they mattered
Like I wasn’t already behind

He saw something I didn’t know how to be
Like a future I couldn’t yet see

But I was writing from the end of things
Where the cranes lean down over empty rings
Where the harbor forgets what it used to bring
And everything leaves before it can sing
He said, “you’re still in the middle, don’t go”
But I only knew how the endings grow
So I walked where the cold wind cuts and stings
Writing from the end of things

My essay came back marked in red ink
Not answers—just questions that burned
“Why do you write like it’s already over?”
Like hope was a lesson I never learned
In the hall with the smell of wet coats
I told him the town was dissolving
That you can’t call it “future” or “home”
When both of them stop evolving

Outside, the fjord wind was sharp as a truth
And the ferry was dying in view
The bell rang for classrooms long empty
Like it didn’t know what else to do
He said I was wrong in a whisper
I said he was wrong in return
And the space between what we were saying
Was the only thing starting to burn

“I believed you would stay open,” he said
And that broke something I couldn’t name
Not anger, not grief, not forgiveness
Just the weight of an unwanted frame
I told him I wouldn’t be shaped
By a hope that wasn’t my own
And I left him there standing in weather
Like a lesson I learned alone

Final Chorus
Now I’m writing from the end of things
Where the gulls outnumber everything
Where the road forgets where it’s meant to lead
And the sea keeps taking what it needs
He said “stay here,” I said “I can’t know”
So I walked where the cold winds go
Past the bakery, closed and still ringing
Writing from the end of things

And maybe the town still remembers my name
In the rust and the salt and the rain
But I only remember the leaving
And the way it keeps sounding the same

songs.scenario: a long drive we took together with my younger self, in a town that's barely there anymore
songs.story:
We left before sunrise, when the world still felt unfinished. The car smelled faintly of cold upholstery and the last traces of pine air freshener that had long since given up trying to hide the oil and dust. You were already in the passenger seat, though I remember thinking you looked too small for it, knees pulled up slightly, fingers tracing invisible patterns on the fogged glass.

The road out of town was the kind that forgets itself in straight lines. Fields on either side were flattened into pale winter tones—stubble, frost, dull green hedges. Every so often a farmhouse would appear, then drift away behind us like a thought not fully formed. We didn’t talk much at first. The engine did what it could to fill the silence, a steady, tired hum.

You asked if the place we were going still existed. I said yes, but I wasn’t entirely sure I meant it.

By late morning we turned off onto a narrower road, cracked asphalt stitching itself through dunes and scrub. The sea appeared without announcement—just suddenly there, slate-colored and restless, pressing against a shore that looked like it had stopped trying to resist it years ago.

The town came next, though “town” is generous. A few low buildings leaning into each other for support. A shuttered grocery with faded blue paint. A bus stop sign bent at the neck, its timetable long erased by weather and time. The name of the place had almost peeled off the wooden welcome sign; I could only make out the first letter and a ghost of the rest.

You got out before I fully stopped the engine.

The wind off the water was sharp enough to taste—salt and rust and something metallic, like old coins. We walked down what used to be the main street. Grass grew through the cracks in the pavement, softening everything, as if the land was slowly deciding to take it back.

You ran your hand along a wall where posters still clung in layers: a lost festival, a missing dog, a political promise no one had come to collect. I watched your face as you read them, watched you trying to map meaning onto something that no longer held still.

That’s when you asked the question I had been avoiding since we left: “Is this what happens?”

I didn’t answer right away. A gull cried somewhere overhead, and the sound felt too loud for such an empty place.

We stood in front of what might have been a café once. The door was gone, just a dark opening into dust and broken chairs. Inside, light came in sideways through the warped windows, cutting the air into pale slices.

“I thought things stayed,” you said quietly. Not accusing, just trying to understand the rules that had changed without warning.

That was the turning point, though I didn’t recognize it until later. I had been carrying the idea that you were following me, that I was showing you forward. But standing there, I realized it was the other way around—you were still capable of being surprised by endings. I had learned them too thoroughly.

We didn’t stay long after that. The car d
songs.lyrics:
We left before the sun had learned our names,
When the sky was only charcoal and flame,
Your knees up high in the passenger seat,
Tracing lost worlds on a fogged-up sheet.
The car held silence like a worn-out coat,
And the engine hummed like a half-true note,
Fields on both sides turned to ash and bone,
And I wasn’t sure if we were going home.

You asked me soft, like you were afraid,
“Does the place we’re going still see its name?”
I said yes, though it slipped through my hands,
Like trying to hold onto shifting sand.

And the road goes on where the stories fade,
Through the rusted light of a world decayed,
Where even the sign can’t remember its face,
Just a letter left in an empty space.
Oh, we were chasing something we couldn’t keep,
Across waking hours that never sleep,
And I didn’t know it then—but I see it now,
Some places don’t wait, they just fade out.

The sea arrived like a sudden thought,
Cold and heavy with all it’s not,
A slate-gray mirror breaking the shore,
Like it had been here forevermore.
The town leaned in like it might collapse,
With shuttered doors and collapsed maps,
And grass grew up through cracked-up stone,
Like the earth was reclaiming its own.

You touched the walls where the posters peeled,
Lost names and promises time revealed,
And I watched your face try to translate,
A language that had already sealed its fate.

And the road goes on where the stories fade,
Through the rusted light of a world decayed,
Where even the sign can’t remember its face,
Just a letter left in an empty space.
Oh, we were chasing something we couldn’t keep,
Across waking hours that never sleep,
And I didn’t know it then—but I see it now,
Some places don’t wait, they just fade out.

You asked, “Is this what happens?”
Like the world had rules we missed,
And a gull cried out like an answer,
But it never quite existed.
I thought I was the one leading forward,
A map in my steady hand,
But you were the one still learning
How endings learn to stand.

Final Chorus
So the road goes on where the stories fade,
Past the edge of the choices we never made,
And the town just breathes in its slow decay,
Like it’s been forgetting itself every day.
Oh, we were only passing through the deep,
Where the ocean and memory never sleep,
And I didn’t know it then—but I see it now,
You were the one who knew how to bow.

We didn’t stay long after that…
The car just… kept going.

songs.scenario: the day I forgave them with my old bandmate, in a cramped city apartment
songs.lyrics:
The radiator coughed like it knew our names
Old amps in the corner, still carrying blame
You were tuning silence, I was counting cracks
In the ceiling where the past kept crawling back

Coffee on the windowsill, cold and half-spilled
Two ghosts in a room that neither of us filled
You said “we should talk” like it weighed too much
And I laughed at how time still pretends it’s enough

We didn’t have room for the truth to be loud
So it fell to the carpet, soft and unbound

In that cramped city apartment, we finally broke the line
Let the years fall apart between your words and mine
No stage, no crowd, just the hum of the street
And the sound of two people learning to breathe
I didn’t need a reason, you didn’t need to explain
We just let go of the weight we both couldn’t name
In that cramped city apartment, we forgave what we became

Your guitar case opened like an old confession
Strings out of tune but still holding tension
You played a chord that almost felt like home
Like a version of us we abandoned alone

I sat on the floor with my back to the wall
Tracing the moment before the fall
You said “I was angry,” I said “me too”
And the silence between us finally told the truth

We didn’t fix it, we didn’t pretend
We just stopped pretending it had to end

In that cramped city apartment, we finally broke the line
Let the years fall apart between your words and mine
No stage, no crowd, just the hum of the street
And the sound of two people learning to breathe
I didn’t need a reason, you didn’t need to explain
We just let go of the weight we both couldn’t name
In that cramped city apartment, we forgave what we became

Maybe forgiveness isn’t a door swinging wide
Maybe it’s just sitting still on the other side
With the mess still there, but lighter somehow
Like we don’t have to carry it now

Final Chorus
In that cramped city apartment, we finally broke the line
Not to go back, just to leave it behind
No stage, no crowd, just the hum of the street
And the sound of two people learning to breathe
I didn’t need a reason, you didn’t need to explain
We just let go of the weight we both couldn’t name
In that cramped city apartment, we forgave what we became

The radiator still coughs in the same tired way
But it doesn’t sound like it’s trying to say
Anything we’re not ready to hear again
Just the quiet of us, at the end of the end

If you want, I can also reshape this into a specific genre (indie rock, emo, folk, post-punk) or match it to a melody structure your band used before.

songs.scenario: the fight that ended it with a teacher who believed in me, in the mountains in summer
songs.lyrics:
We climbed those roads where the pine trees bend,
Where the sky felt close like an old best friend,
You said my name like it meant something more,
Like I wasn’t just who I was before.

You marked my pages, slow and sure,
Told me doubt was just a door,
And I believed you in that light,
Under the long blue stretch of height.

But words can shift like weather does,
Soft at first, then turn to dust…

And it was summer in the mountains when it broke apart,
A quiet kind of thunder in the back of my heart,
We didn’t shout like people do in the films or the dark,
Just two truths colliding, leaving deeper marks.
You said I had to try, I said I already did,
And something in that moment split and slid,
Now I still hear your voice when the wind runs high—
But I don’t know if it’s truth or goodbye.

You saw a future I couldn’t see,
Said I was more than what I’d ever be,
But pressure grows like stone in the sun,
And even mountains come undone.

I spoke too sharp, you stood too still,
Like you’d already climbed every hill,
And I wonder now in the quiet air,
If either of us was really there.

Did we fall, or just drift off course,
Two strong currents losing force…

And it was summer in the mountains when it broke apart,
A quiet kind of thunder in the back of my heart,
We didn’t shout like people do in the films or the dark,
Just two truths colliding, leaving deeper marks.
You said I had to grow, I said I couldn’t stay,
And neither of us knew what to throw away,
Now every winding trail feels like it leads back to you—
To the version of me I never knew.

If I could go back to that ridge in July,
I’d let the silence sit between us and try,
Not to win, not to fall, not to prove anything at all…
Just listen before the echo turned into a wall.

Final Chorus
And it was summer in the mountains when it slipped from our hands,
Like water through fingers, like shifting sands,
No final goodbye, just a change in the air,
A lesson too heavy for either to bear.
You believed in me more than I believed in myself—
Now that belief sits on a distant shelf,
And I carry it still, though it cuts like a line,
Through the part of me you once called “mine.”

If you want, I can reshape it into a more specific genre (folk, indie rock, spoken word, etc.), or make it more direct, angrier, or more nostalgic.

songs.scenario: a phone call that came at 3am with an ex I still think about, back in the old neighborhood
songs.lyrics:
Three a.m. and the streetlight’s humming,
Like it knows somethin’ I don’t wanna know.
That same old number on the screen is coming,
From a life I said I let go slow.

I was half asleep in a rented quiet,
Different town, different kind of blue.
But that ring cut through like a train through silence,
And all at once I was back with you.

Back in the old neighborhood, where the pavement knows my name,
Where we carved our forever in the cracks of a windowpane.
I told myself I was doing good, I was doing all I could,
But I answered when I shouldn’t have, at 3 a.m. in the old neighborhood.

You said, “I know it’s late, I shouldn’t be calling,”
Your voice still had that worn-out spark.
Like a porchlight flickering, barely stalling
The way we fell apart in the dark.

You didn’t say you missed me outright,
But it lived between every word you chose.
Like a ghost in a kitchen light,
Like a story nobody quite closes.

Back in the old neighborhood, where the pavement knows my name,
Where we carved our forever in the cracks of a windowpane.
I told myself I was doing good, I was doing all I could,
But I answered when I shouldn’t have, at 3 a.m. in the old neighborhood.

Now the silence feels heavier than it did before,
Like it’s got your shape still in the hall.
And I don’t know what I was hoping for,
But I hear your voice and I lose it all.

Three a.m. fades into morning light,
I’m staring at a coffee gone cold.
And I swear I left you that other night,
But some goodbyes don’t do what they’re told.

Back in the old neighborhood, where the pavement knows my name,
Where the past still hums like a worn-out flame.
I told myself I was doing good, I was doing all I could,
But I’m still there with you sometimes… at 3 a.m. in the old neighborhood.

songs.scenario: the summer everything changed with my estranged father, in the suburbs in the rain
songs.lyrics:
It was summer in the suburbs, heat stuck to the street,
The air was thick with silence we never learned to meet.
Your name sat in my phone like a word I couldn’t say,
Like a house with all the lights on, but nobody came to stay.

The neighbors cut their grass like nothing’s ever wrong,
While I rehearsed forgiveness like a half-forgotten song.
You showed up in a memory I thought I buried deep,
Standing at the edge of something neither of us could keep.

And I don’t know what I wanted,
Just not to feel this small—
In a driveway full of distance,
Where you never really called.

It was summer, everything changed in the rain,
In the suburbs where we learned each other’s pain.
You were a stranger with my last name in your eyes,
And I was every unsaid word you couldn’t apologize.
The sky cracked open like it knew what we became—
That summer, nothing stayed the same.

You leaned against the car like a man I almost knew,
Talking in the weather like we had something to do.
I counted all the reasons I should turn and walk away,
But something in the thunder made me stay a little way.

You asked about my life like it was news from far away,
Like I didn’t grow from absence you were supposed to stay.
And I laughed at how the timing always cuts too deep,
How even in the daylight, some ghosts don’t fall asleep.

And every drop between us
Was louder than we spoke,
Like the past came down in water
And turned everything to smoke.

It was summer, everything changed in the rain,
In the suburbs where we learned each other’s pain.
You were a stranger with my last name in your eyes,
And I was every unsaid word you couldn’t apologize.
The sky cracked open like it knew what we became—
That summer, nothing stayed the same.

And I don’t know if we were healing
Or just learning how to break,
But the silence felt like something
We were finally forced to take.
In the roar of tired thunder,
You reached out, then pulled away—
Like even love remembered
It was too late to stay.

Final Chorus
It was summer, everything changed in the rain,
In the suburbs where we traced the lines of pain.
No ending, no beginning, just the weight of what we knew—
That some roads don’t return you to who you thought you’d be with you.
And the sky kept falling like it always had to do—
That summer, I lost and found you.

Now every time it rains in quiet suburban light,
I hear that broken summer folding into night.
And I don’t know if I forgive you or the storm,
But I remember how it felt when everything was torn.

songs.scenario: the day I forgave them with a teacher who believed in me, out on the prairie
songs.lyrics:
Out where the prairie learns the shape of sky,
Where the wind remembers every reason why,
I stood with a silence I’d carried too long,
Like a book with a page where I didn’t belong.

You said, “There’s more in you than what they name,”
Like chalk on a board that rewrites shame,
A teacher’s voice steady as the turning sun,
Saying, “You don’t have to become what you’ve run from.”

And I forgave them, not like a spark or a fight,
But like dusk letting go of the fight with light,
Not because it was easy, not because it was right,
But because I was tired of carrying the night.

We walked where the prairie forgets its end,
Where broken things start to mend again,
You didn’t fix me—you just stayed near,
Long enough for the truth to appear.

And the wind said nothing, but it understood,
How anger can wear the shape of “good,”
How letting it go isn’t calling it small,
But choosing to stand up after it all.

So I forgave them, with dust on my shoes,
With a heart that finally loosened its noose,
And you, like a margin note in my life’s long page,
Showed me the courage it takes to disengage.

Out on the prairie, where endings begin,
I learned what it means to let light in,
Not because they changed, not because I did,
But because I finally stopped being hid.

songs.scenario: a phone call that came at 3am with someone I met once on a train, in the suburbs in the rain
songs.lyrics:
It was a quiet night in the suburbs, neon fading slow
Streetlights dripping on the pavement like they already know
I met you once on a train ride, windows blurred with grey
Two strangers in the rain talking just to pass the day

You said your stop came early, I said mine came too
We laughed like it was nothing, like we always do

But nothing ever really ends the way it starts
It just hides in the corners of two wandering hearts

Then the phone lit up at 3:07
Like a message falling straight down from heaven
Your voice came in like a song I forgot I knew
Saying “I don’t know why, but I had to call you”
Now I’m wide awake in this quiet rain
In the suburbs where nothing ever changes
But everything changed the night you came through
At 3:07, I came back to you

You said you missed the sound of a train pulling in
Like it carried all the versions of where you’ve been
I said I still remember how you smiled at the glass
Like you were chasing something that was never meant to last

Now I’m standing by my window, counting every drop
Wondering if a moment like that ever really stops

Or does it wait in the dark just to pull us back
When the world goes quiet and the signal cracks

Then the phone lit up at 3:07
Like a message falling straight down from heaven
Your voice came in like a song I forgot I knew
Saying “I don’t know why, but I had to call you”
Now I’m breathing in that electric rain
In the suburbs where nothing ever changes
But everything changed the night you came through
At 3:07, I came back to you

Maybe it’s chance, maybe it’s something more
Maybe we’re just echoes looking for a shore
But I swear that train still moves through my mind
And your voice breaks through every borderline

Final Chorus
So if you call again at 3:07
I’ll answer like it’s some kind of heaven
No questions asked, just a familiar truth
That I was always waiting there for you
In this quiet world where nothing stays the same
Except the way your voice still knows my name
And every midnight feels brand new
At 3:07, I come back to you

Suburban rain, fading slow
And somehow now I know…
Some strangers don’t stay strangers for long
They just wait for a call at 3:07 to come along.

songs.scenario: an ordinary afternoon I somehow never forgot with my older brother, back in the old neighborhood
songs.lyrics:
The light was tired, leaning on the street,
Dust in the air, slow in the heat,
Your voice half-lost in a passing car,
Nothing was close, nothing too far.
Just a bottle sweating on the step,
And time pretending it hadn’t left.

And I didn’t know then what it would mean,
Just another page in a quiet routine—

But it stays like a song that won’t come clean,
An ordinary afternoon I’ve never fully seen,
Back where the pavement knew our names,
And nothing yet had changed its shape.
You’re living it now, I’m watching it bend,
Into the place I can’t go again.

My older brother threw stones at the sky,
Like he could teach the clouds to reply,
We laughed at nothing, dared the air,
Like growing up wasn’t waiting there.
The neighborhood held us like a secret vow,
We didn’t know what we know now.

Every small thing felt infinite then,
Like it would always be that way again—

But it stays like a song that won’t come clean,
An ordinary afternoon I’ve never fully seen,
Back where the pavement knew our names,
And nothing yet had changed its shape.
You’re living it now, I’m watching it bend,
Into the place I can’t go again.

Tell me if the street still smells like rain,
If the same old dogs still know your name,
If the light still falls the same slow way,
Or if it left with us the day we stayed.

Final chorus
It stays like a song that won’t come clean,
Not just yours, not just mine—somewhere in between,
A borrowed hour that never lets go,
Of a life we didn’t yet know.
You’re in it now, I’m far from the frame,
But it still answers when I hear your name.

Just an ordinary afternoon,
That somehow never ended for me too.

songs.scenario: moving to a new city completely alone with a neighbor I grew up next to, on a cross-country highway
songs.lyrics:
I packed my life in cardboard lines,
taped up memories, left the light behind,
the street I knew like the back of my hand
now shrinking in the rearview glass.

And you were there with your coffee cold,
same old jokes that we used to know,
two kids from doors that barely closed right,
now chasing distance into the night.

We didn’t say much at the start of the drive,
just watched the world turn into white lines.

We’re rolling down a cross-country highway,
two ghosts from the same hometown skyway,
you next to me, the only thing I recognize
in a city we haven’t met yet, still in our minds.
New names, new streets, new reasons to try—
but it’s just us under the same wide sky.

Gas station lights like borrowed stars,
we count the miles in the hum of the car,
you talk about dreams you never outgrew,
I pretend I’ve got some too.

And I wonder if we’re running or just late,
from everything we couldn’t change back home,
or if this is what it means to grow—
to leave together but still feel alone.

The farther we go, the less we speak,
but somehow it still feels like you’re next to me.

We’re rolling down a cross-country highway,
two ghosts from the same hometown skyway,
you next to me, the only thing I recognize
in a city we haven’t met yet, still in our minds.
New names, new streets, new reasons to try—
but it’s just us under the same wide sky.

Maybe the city won’t feel like home at all,
maybe we’ll learn to stand or fall,
but right now the tires keep singing our song,
“keep going, keep going, keep moving along.”

Final Chorus
We’re rolling down a cross-country highway,
leaving everything we knew in the backseat, far away,
you next to me, like a memory I can touch,
the only constant in a world changing too much.
New life, new noise, new names to try—
but it’s still us under the same wide sky.

And when we arrive and the engine dies,
I hope we still know each other’s eyes.

If you want, I can also turn this into a more specific genre (indie folk, country, pop, rap) or tighten it into a radio-length chorus hook.

songs.scenario: the morning I knew it was over with my estranged father, out on the prairie
songs.story:
The prairie was still holding the night when I stepped out of the truck, the grass stiff with frost that crunched like thin glass under my boots. The horizon was only a suggestion—gray melting into paler gray—and the wind carried that dry, mineral smell of open land that never quite warms, even in summer. My father was already there, a dark shape a few yards away, leaning on the fence line like he had been waiting longer than I had been alive.

We didn’t greet each other. We never really had words for arrival anymore.

I remember the way his hands looked first—creased, swollen at the knuckles, nails rimmed with dirt that never quite washed out. He tapped the fence post with a slow, irregular rhythm, like he was counting something I couldn’t hear. A herd of cattle shifted somewhere beyond the rise, their bells faint and distant, like memory trying not to be remembered.

“You came,” he said finally, not turning to look at me.

It wasn’t a question. It wasn’t gratitude either.

I nodded anyway, though he still wasn’t looking. The wind pulled at my jacket, slid cold fingers down my neck. I thought of all the versions of this moment I had rehearsed on the drive out—accusations, explanations, something sharp enough to finally cut through the distance between us. But out here, under this enormous indifferent sky, they felt like objects I’d left in another life.

He spat into the dirt. “Fence needs fixing past the draw.”

“I saw it,” I said.

Silence stretched. Not awkward, just complete.

When he finally turned, I saw how much smaller he looked than I remembered. Not frail exactly, just… reduced, like the prairie had been quietly sanding him down for years. His eyes met mine and held there, steady, almost curious, as if I were a weather pattern he was trying to forecast.

That was when it happened—not loudly, not with any clear rupture. Just a settling. A certainty arriving without permission.

I realized I was no longer waiting for him to become someone else. Not waiting for an apology, or a reckoning, or even recognition. There was only this man, this fence, this cold morning, and a lifetime of things that would never be said.

He turned back toward the horizon. “Wind’s shifting,” he muttered.

I knew then it was over—not the relationship exactly, but the illusion that it might still become something different. The prairie swallowed the words I never said, and I let it.
songs.lyrics:
Frost on the prairie, breaking glass under boot,
Truck door sighs open in the half-light mute.
Horizon dissolves where the gray meets the gray,
And the wind says your name but it never stays.

You were already there like a shadow held still,
Leaning on the fence line, older than will.
Counting out time with a post and a hand,
Like the earth keeps a ledger no one understands.

You didn’t look up when I stepped to the ground,
Just the old kind of silence that swallows all sound.
I carried a dozen sharp words in my chest,
But they froze in the air and refused to confess.

“Fence needs fixing past the draw,” you said low,
Like a truth that was always the only thing known.
I said, “I saw it,” like that meant anything more,
Than two separate lives on the same frozen shore.

And the wind keeps shifting, but nothing returns,
Just the prairie that listens and quietly learns.
No beginning, no ending, just distance made plain,
Like a fence line that sags but still holds in the same.
I thought I came here to change what we are—
But the sky doesn’t argue, it just is, from afar.

Your hands were the first thing that time made unknown,
Knuckles like stones in a field overgrown.
You tapped out a rhythm I couldn’t decode,
Like a language the land taught you long down the road.

A herd in the distance, bells thin as regret,
Like memories pretending they’re not done yet.
And I watched you turn once, not softened, just small,
Like the prairie had been sanding you down after all.

Not a rupture, not thunder, not fire in the sky,
Just a moment when waiting decides it will die.
No apology coming, no reckoning near,
Just the cold, and the fence, and the truth becoming clear.

Final Chorus
So the wind keeps shifting, and I let it pass,
Let the prairie take words that were never built to last.
You face the horizon like it knows your name,
And I stop trying to turn you into someone else again.
It’s not love that is ending, not hate that remains—
Just the quiet of fields after forgetting your name.

songs.scenario: moving to a new city completely alone with my grandmother, out on the prairie
songs.lyrics:
Prairie Lines

We packed the house into a rusted trailer
Boxes full of ghosts and hand-me-down names
Grandma drove while I stared through the window
Watching our old life fade into the rain

The highway stretched like a question with no answer
Telephone poles counting out the miles
Every town got smaller in the rearview mirror
Till all I saw was endless sky

And the wind kept saying
"Keep going, keep going"
Even when I wanted to turn around

Now it's just me and my grandmother
In a city nobody knows
Built on dust and stubborn dreams
Where the cold wind always blows

I'm learning how to stand alone
Under these prairie stars
Trying to find where I belong
With a heart full of scars

Out here the silence hits harder
Than any goodbye back home
It's just me and my grandmother
And we're making this place our own

The streets are wide but they still feel crowded
When you're the stranger everyone sees
Every face already knows somebody
While I'm memorizing names and streets

Grandma says, "Give it time, kid"
While she's planting flowers by the door
Like she's certain roots can grow anywhere
Even on unfamiliar floors

And the wind keeps saying
"Keep going, keep going"
Like it knows something I don't

Now it's just me and my grandmother
In a city nobody knows
Built on dust and stubborn dreams
Where the cold wind always blows

I'm learning how to stand alone
Under these prairie stars
Trying to find where I belong
With a heart full of scars

Out here the silence hits harder
Than any goodbye back home
It's just me and my grandmother
And we're making this place our own

Some nights I miss the streets that raised me
The friends I left behind
But the horizon here is endless
And maybe so am I

Maybe every lonely mile
Was leading somewhere new
Maybe being lost is how you learn
What you're supposed to do

Final Chorus
Now it's just me and my grandmother
With the prairie in our veins
Chasing little pieces of tomorrow
Through the sunshine and the rain

I'm not the same kid who left home
With a suitcase full of doubt
These open fields and empty roads
Are slowly figuring me out

And the wind still sings the same old song
Across the rolling plains
It's just me and my grandmother
And we're not afraid to change

Telephone poles and prairie lines
Stretching farther than I can see
A new city under a bigger sky
Making a little more room for me

songs.scenario: coming back home after years away with my mother, on a cross-country highway
songs.lyrics:
Back Across the Miles

The interstate was humming like an old familiar tune,
Fields rolled by in patches of green and dusty June.
My mother held the wheel with both her weathered hands,
Tracing roads we'd left behind across a dozen states and lands.

The years had scattered pieces of the boy I used to be,
In cities full of strangers and dreams that wouldn't sleep.
But somewhere past the county line, beneath that endless sky,
I saw my reflection in her eyes as the miles rolled by.

We're coming back home, back where the rivers bend,
Back to the front porch stories and the roads that never end.
The world can pull you far away, but it can't erase your name,
And every mile beside my mother brings me closer just the same.
We're coming back home, after all these years gone by,
Two shadows on a highway underneath a painted sky.

She pointed out old landmarks that I'd nearly forgotten now,
The grain elevator standing proud, the church beyond the plow.
She laughed about my younger days, the trouble and the noise,
The stubborn dreams that carried me beyond those hometown joys.

I listened to her stories like a traveler hears a song,
Finding pieces of myself in words I'd missed for far too long.
The setting sun stretched golden light across the dashboard glow,
And every mile we traveled felt like healing what I owed.

We're coming back home, back where the rivers bend,
Back to the front porch stories and the roads that never end.
The world can pull you far away, but it can't erase your name,
And every mile beside my mother brings me closer just the same.
We're coming back home, after all these years gone by,
Two shadows on a highway underneath a painted sky.

There's something about the long road,
Something about the way she sings,
Like every cracked and faded memory
Still has a pair of wings.
And the farther that we've traveled,
The more I understand,
Home was never just a place—
It was her guiding hand.

Final Chorus
We're coming back home, where the old oak meets the lane,
Where the screen door keeps on talking every time it starts to rain.
I've chased horizons all my life, crossed mountains, seas, and plains,
But riding here beside my mother, I know what still remains.
We're coming back home, and the lights are shining bright,
Two hearts on a cross-country highway driving into the night.

The interstate grew quiet as the hometown came in view,
And she smiled the way she always did when dreams finally came true.
After years of being lost out there beneath the open dome,
I found my way back with my mother—
Back home.

songs.scenario: the first winter on my own with an ex I still think about, in a small rust-belt town
songs.lyrics:
First Winter Alone

The mill stacks stand like skeletons
Against a frozen evening sky
Main Street's lit in tired neon
Half the bulbs have already died

The diner closes early now
The church bell cracks the dark in two
Every road is lined with dirty snow
And somehow everything leads back to you

I keep finding your shadow
In places you've never been

It's my first winter on my own
In this rust-belt town of rust and stone
The river froze, the factories too
But nothing here gets over you

The wind comes off the empty yards
Rattles every house and car
I tell myself you're long gone now
But you're still everywhere somehow

The hardware store put Christmas up
Before Thanksgiving even came
And every string of colored lights
Just makes the streets look more the same

I pass the bar where we'd waste Fridays
Talking big about getting out
Now the jukebox hums through half-filled rooms
And nobody's saying much aloud

Everybody keeps moving
At least that's how it seems

It's my first winter on my own
In this rust-belt town of rust and stone
The river froze, the factories too
But nothing here gets over you

The wind comes off the empty yards
Rattles every house and car
I tell myself you're long gone now
But you're still everywhere somehow

Maybe that's the trick of winter
Everything gets stripped down bare
The trees, the fields, the old brick buildings
And all the things we used to share

Maybe by the thaw I'll miss you less
Maybe spring will change the view
But tonight the snow keeps falling
And every flake comes back to you

Final Chorus
It's my first winter on my own
Watching headlights drift through falling snow
The town keeps shrinking year by year
But your memory stays crystal clear

The river froze, the factories too
The sky turned steel, the nights turned blue
And if this cold ever lets me go
I don't think I'll be the first to know

The mill stacks stand like skeletons
Against the pale and silent moon
This town and I are holding on
To things that aren't coming back soon

songs.scenario: the summer everything changed with a teacher who believed in me, out on the prairie
songs.lyrics:
The Summer Everything Changed

The wind rolled slow through the prairie grass
Like it knew something I didn't know yet
June stretched wide beneath a painted sky
And I was carrying every doubt I had left

I kept my head down, kept my dreams small
Like a fence line ending at the county road
Then you looked past all the grades and noise
And saw a place I hadn't dared to go

That was the summer everything changed
Out where the horizon called my name
Under endless blue and fields of gold
You taught me I was more than what I'd been told

When the world felt bigger than I could be
You believed before I could believe in me
And the prairie carried that truth like a flame
That was the summer everything changed

We'd talk after class while the daylight lingered
Long enough to make the whole town glow
You said, "Questions matter more than answers"
And suddenly I wanted more to know

The meadowlarks sang from the telephone wires
Dust rose behind every passing truck
Somewhere between those open spaces
I learned hope wasn't just a matter of luck

That was the summer everything changed
Out where the horizon called my name
Under endless blue and fields of gold
You taught me I was more than what I'd been told

When the world felt bigger than I could be
You believed before I could believe in me
And the prairie carried that truth like a flame
That was the summer everything changed

Years have passed like storms across the plains
But some lessons never fade away
Every road I've traveled started there
In the light of those long July days

Final Chorus
That was the summer everything changed
The summer I stopped being afraid
And whenever doubt comes back again
I remember who I was back then

A teacher's faith, a wide-open sky
A dream that finally learned to fly
And the prairie still whispers your name
From the summer everything changed

songs.scenario: a phone call that came at 3am with my sister, on a cross-country highway
songs.lyrics:
Three A.M. Highway Call

The white lines rolled beneath my wheels
Like a ribbon through the dark
Somewhere past the state line signs
With a coffee and a tired heart

The radio was whispering
An old song I half-knew by name
When my phone lit up the dashboard blue
And nothing felt the same

Three in the morning
No one calls that late
So I held my breath a second
Before I touched the screen and prayed

It was my sister on the line
Three a.m. beneath the stars
I was chasing dawn across the country
She was crying from miles apart
The highway stretched forever
The moon hung cold and small
But suddenly I wasn't alone
On that three a.m. highway call

She said, "Are you still driving?"
I said, "Yeah, somewhere out west"
She laughed through tears and silence
Said, "I just needed your voice, I guess"

So I told her about truck-stop lights
And the mountains I couldn't see
How the world feels twice as lonely
When it's only you and the speed

The tires kept on humming
Like a steady old refrain
And every mile between us
Felt a little less like pain

It was my sister on the line
Three a.m. beneath the stars
I was chasing dawn across the country
She was crying from miles apart
The highway stretched forever
The moon hung cold and small
But suddenly I wasn't alone
On that three a.m. highway call

Maybe that's what family is
A voice that finds you in the night
When the road gets long and empty
And you can't quite see the light

A thousand miles can't break the thread
That ties our stories all together
Sometimes love arrives disguised
As bad timing and stormy weather

Final Chorus
It was my sister on the line
Three a.m. beneath the stars
I was chasing dawn across the country
She was healing old scars
The sun was rising slowly
Painting gold across it all
And I carried her with me for miles
From that three a.m. highway call

The white lines rolled beneath my wheels
The morning finally came
But every time my phone lights up
I still remember her name.

songs.scenario: the summer everything changed with my old bandmate, in the mountains in summer
songs.lyrics:
The Summer Everything Changed

The road curled up through the pines and stone
Two beat-up guitars and a place we'd borrowed
Windows down, singing over the engine noise
Like we owned tomorrow

The mornings smelled of cedar and rain
The afternoons stretched gold for miles
You'd sketch new songs on the backs of maps
And swear they'd last a while

We thought every fire would keep burning
We thought every trail came back around

But that was the summer everything changed
High in the mountains where we learned our names
Playing to the stars, chasing echoes through the dark
Thinking we'd never drift apart

The river kept running, the season moved on
Somehow the days got too small to hold on
I still hear your voice when the evening comes around
From the summer everything changed

There was a lookout over the valley floor
Where we'd watch the thunderstorms roll in
Talking like the future was a door
We could kick right open

And every night we'd play until dawn
Our fingers aching, hearts on fire
Not knowing how fast a dream can grow
Or how fast it can expire

The sky was wide enough for believing
The years ahead were ours to claim

But that was the summer everything changed
High in the mountains where we learned our names
Playing to the stars, chasing echoes through the dark
Thinking we'd never drift apart

The river kept running, the season moved on
Somehow the days got too small to hold on
I still hear your voice when the evening comes around
From the summer everything changed

Now the cabins fade into memory
The trails are overgrown in my mind
But every note we left among those peaks
Still survives the passing time

Maybe we were never meant to stay
Maybe that's what made it true
Some things only shine because they vanish
Like that summer, me and you

Final Chorus
Yeah, that was the summer everything changed
The mountains are the same, but nothing remains
Of the kids who swore forever underneath that endless sky
Except a song that never died

And when the sun falls behind the ridge again
I swear I can almost see us then
Two old bandmates laughing in the fading mountain light
From the summer everything changed

songs.scenario: the summer everything changed with a neighbor I grew up next to, in the mountains in summer
songs.story:
The summer everything changed began with the smell of sun-warmed pine needles and dust rising from the dirt road between our cabins.

I was sixteen, and Eli had lived next door for as long as I could remember. Our families spent every summer in the mountains, in a narrow valley where the mornings were cold enough for sweaters and the afternoons shimmered with heat. We had grown up building forts in the woods, catching grasshoppers in mason jars, and daring each other to jump into the freezing creek that cut through the meadow below.

That July, something felt different before either of us said a word about it.

Every morning I heard the screen door of his family's cabin slam around the same time. I'd look up from the porch and see him crossing the yard with a fishing rod over one shoulder. He had gotten taller over the winter. His voice sounded lower. Even the familiar way he pushed his hair out of his eyes seemed suddenly unfamiliar.

One afternoon we hiked to an alpine lake we'd visited dozens of times before. The trail climbed through stands of fir trees, and the air smelled sharp and green. Cicadas buzzed in the heat. Sweat dampened the back of my shirt beneath my backpack.

At the lake, we sat on a granite boulder eating bruised peaches we'd carried in our packs. Juice ran down our wrists. Across the water, the mountains reflected perfectly in the still surface.

Neither of us talked much.

Then clouds rolled over the ridge faster than expected.

By the time we started back, thunder echoed between the peaks. Rain arrived in a sudden sheet, cold and heavy. We ran beneath a rocky overhang and pressed against the stone wall, laughing and breathless.

The storm darkened everything.

For a long moment, neither of us spoke. Rain hammered the rocks outside. I could smell wet earth and the mineral scent of the stone beside us.

Then Eli said quietly, "We're probably not going to keep doing this forever, are we?"

I knew exactly what he meant. Not the hike. Not the storm.

The summers.

The certainty that we'd always return here unchanged.

I remember looking at him and feeling something crack open inside me. Growing up had always seemed like a distant event. Suddenly it was standing right there with us beneath that overhang.

"No," I said.

We sat listening to the rain.

When the storm finally passed, the forest looked transformed. Every pine needle glittered. The creek below roared louder than before. The air felt washed clean.

We walked home in silence, but it wasn't the old comfortable silence of childhood. It carried the weight of knowing things end.

That was the last summer before college, jobs, and different cities pulled us apart. I still visit those mountains sometimes. The cabins are smaller than I remember. The road is paved now.

But whenever I smell rain on warm pine trees, I'm back under that rocky ledge, sixteen years old, realizing that the real turning point wasn't falling in love or saying goodbye.

It was understanding, for the f
songs.lyrics:
The summer started with the pine trees breathing heat
Dust rose behind our bikes along that old dirt street
You slammed the screen door every morning just the same
But somehow when I saw you, nothing felt unchanged

We'd spent a hundred summers chasing dragonflies
Building kingdoms in the woods beneath the mountain skies
But something in the air had shifted without sound
Like the earth was turning slowly underneath the ground

And I didn't have the words
For what I felt back then
Only knew the world was wider
Than it had ever been

When the rain came down on the mountainside
And the thunder rolled through the pines
We hid beneath that rocky ledge
Watching childhood drift by

You said, "We're not gonna do this forever, are we?"
Like you already knew somehow
And the storm kept falling all around us
While the years came rushing in

I thought growing up was somewhere far away
Till the rain came down that day

We climbed the trail to the lake we'd always known
Bruised peaches in our backpacks, granite for a throne
The mountains stood reflected in the water clear and still
Like a photograph pretending time stood still

The wind arrived before the clouds could be explained
Then the sky broke open in a curtain of cold rain
Laughing as we ran for shelter from the gray
Neither one of us the same as yesterday

And the silence between us
Wasn't empty anymore
It carried all the questions
Waiting at the door

When the rain came down on the mountainside
And the thunder rolled through the pines
We hid beneath that rocky ledge
Watching childhood drift by

You said, "We're not gonna do this forever, are we?"
Like you already knew somehow
And the storm kept falling all around us
While the years came rushing in

I thought growing up was somewhere far away
Till the rain came down that day

Now the road is paved where the dust clouds used to fly
The cabins all seem smaller every time I drive by
You've got your life somewhere beyond these mountain towns
And I carry mine wherever I go now

But sometimes after summer heat
When the evening sky turns gray
The scent of rain on warm pine needles
Takes me back again

Final Chorus
When the rain came down on the mountainside
And the thunder rolled through the pines
I learned that every beautiful thing
Lives for only a little while

It wasn't falling in love
And it wasn't saying goodbye
It was seeing time for what it was
For the very first time

And every storm since then reminds me
How the heart learns somehow
That nothing stays forever unchanged
When the rain comes down

Sun-warmed pine and mountain air
A summer lost somehow
Still sixteen beneath that ledge
When the rain comes down.

songs.scenario: an ordinary afternoon I somehow never forgot with my younger self, in a cramped city apartment
songs.story:
I still remember the apartment because of the window.

It was a narrow place on the fourth floor of a brick building, the kind where every room seemed to serve three purposes at once. The kitchen bled into the living room. The living room became a bedroom at night. The hallway was so tight that two people couldn't pass without turning sideways.

That afternoon, rain had just stopped. The window above the radiator was cracked open an inch, and the air smelled like wet concrete and car exhaust drifting up from the street. Somewhere downstairs, someone was frying onions. The scent floated through the building and settled into everything.

I was sitting cross-legged on the worn carpet, sorting a pile of old photographs my mother had dumped from a shoebox. My younger self—eight years old, all elbows and restless energy—lay on his stomach beside me, drawing spaceships on the back of junk mail.

The television was on with the volume low. Not because we were watching it, but because the apartment felt too small when it was quiet.

I remember the scratch of his pencil. The hiss of the radiator. The occasional splash of tires through puddles four floors below.

For a long time neither of us said much.

Then he picked up one of the photographs.

"Why don't we have pictures of Grandpa when he was old?"

I looked up.

It was such a simple question.

The photo showed my grandfather at maybe twenty-five, standing beside a car that no longer existed. He was smiling into sunlight. Strong. Young.

My younger self waited.

I told him Grandpa died before I was born.

He frowned at the picture as if trying to solve a puzzle.

"So he never met me?"

"No."

"He would've liked me, though."

I laughed a little.

"Yeah. I think he would've."

The room felt different after that.

I don't know why. Maybe because children ask questions without realizing they've opened a door. Maybe because I'd spent years treating my grandfather as a story instead of a person.

I found myself staring at that photograph. At the shape of his smile. The angle of his shoulders. Tiny details I'd never noticed.

For the first time, I understood something that should have been obvious: he had once been exactly as alive as we were in that cramped apartment. He'd heard rain. Smelled dinners cooking. Sat through ordinary afternoons without knowing which ones would matter later.

My younger self had already gone back to drawing.

But I couldn't.

The rainwater on the fire escape outside caught a slant of late sunlight and threw wavering reflections across the ceiling. The apartment glowed gold for a few minutes. Dust drifted through the light like tiny sparks.

Nothing dramatic happened. Nobody arrived. Nobody left.

Yet whenever I think about childhood, I return to that afternoon.

Not because of the photographs themselves, but because it was the moment I first felt time stretching in both directions. A dead man becoming real. A child beside me who wouldn't stay a child. An ordinary room that no longe
songs.lyrics:
The Window on the Fourth Floor

There was a window on the fourth floor
Cracked open just an inch
Rain had washed the city clean
But the streets still smelled of it

Wet concrete and passing cars
Onions cooking down below
Every room was three things at once
In that little place we'd known

The TV hummed a quiet tune
To keep the silence from the walls
I sat sorting photographs
While afternoon grew soft and small

And beside me on the carpet
A boy drew rockets in the mail
Not knowing every ordinary day
Leaves a story in its trail

Time was sitting in that room
Though we didn't know its name
In the hiss of old radiators
In the gold light after rain

A man I'd never met was smiling
From a picture worn and thin
And a child who thought forever lasted
Was right there next to him

Through a window on the fourth floor
The years stretched out and bent
An ordinary afternoon
Became the place they met

He picked up a faded photograph
A young man by a car
Sunlight caught his easy grin
Like he'd never traveled far

He asked me why we had no pictures
Of Grandpa growing old
I told him he was gone before
My own story had been told

He stared down at the image
Like there was something he could find
Then said, "He never got to meet me?"
And I said, "No, not this time."

"He would've liked me, though."

I laughed and shook my head
"Yeah, I think he would've."

And something shifted then

Time was sitting in that room
Though we didn't know its name
In the hiss of old radiators
In the gold light after rain

A man I'd never met was smiling
From a picture worn and thin
And a child who thought forever lasted
Was right there next to him

Through a window on the fourth floor
The years stretched out and bent
An ordinary afternoon
Became the place they met

Rainwater on the fire escape
Caught the falling evening sun
Reflections danced across the ceiling
Dust like sparks in slow motion

Nobody arrived
Nobody said goodbye
But suddenly the world felt wider
Than the rooms could hold inside

He had heard the rain like we did
Smelled dinners cooking in the hall
Lived through days that seemed forgotten
Till memory saved them all

Final Chorus
Time was sitting in that room
Now I know its face somehow
A grandfather becoming real
A child becoming now

And all the things that never happened
Still somehow belong
In a narrow fourth-floor apartment
Where the light stayed just long enough

Through a window on the fourth floor
I can still see where I've been
A dead man, a little boy, and me
All shining in the same thin beam

The rain was gone
The daylight faded slow
Nothing happened that afternoon

And that's why I still go.

songs.scenario: the morning I knew it was over with my grandmother, in the suburbs in the rain
songs.story:
It was the kind of rain that made everything in the suburbs look slightly erased, like someone had taken a damp cloth to the world and blurred the edges of the houses. The rooftops across the street were the color of old pencil lead. Water ran in thin rivers along the curb, carrying last night’s leaves in slow, obedient lines.

I remember how quiet the kitchen was that morning. Not peaceful—just suspended. The radiator ticked in the corner like it was trying to remind us time was still moving. I had left my shoes by the door, soaked through already, and I could smell wet wool rising from my socks.

She was sitting in her chair by the window, the one she refused to stop using even when she stopped being able to get up from it easily. A blanket was folded over her knees, though I don’t think she felt cold anymore in the way I did. There was a cup of tea on the table beside her, untouched, the surface of it gone dull and skin-like.

“Is it still raining?” she asked, though she was looking right at it.

“Yes,” I said. “It’s been raining since dawn.”

She nodded slowly, like that explained something important.

I had been telling myself for weeks that nothing had really changed. That she was just tired, that mornings were harder now, that confusion came and went like weather. But that morning, I noticed her hands resting on the blanket—how still they were. Not weak exactly. Just… finished, as if they had already done everything they were going to do in this life.

When I knelt beside her, she turned her head toward me and studied my face for a long time. I waited for recognition to land the way it used to. Like a light switching on.

Instead, she asked, very softly, “Are you here to fix the window?”

There wasn’t a broken window.

That was the moment it shifted—not with drama, not with tears, but with a strange clarity that felt almost physical, like the air had changed pressure. I understood then that I could stay in the room, but I couldn’t bring her back to me from wherever she was going. The distance between us had already been decided somewhere I hadn’t been invited to.

Outside, the rain kept falling with patient certainty, washing the glass clean over and over again.
songs.lyrics:
It was the kind of rain that blurs the edges of the street,
Turns every roof to pencil lead, every color incomplete.
The suburbs looked half-forgotten, like a dream left out too long,
And the world ran thin and quiet where everything felt wrong.
Water traced the curbside like it knew where it should go,
Carrying last night’s leaves in a patient, drifting flow.

The kitchen held its silence like a breath that won’t release,
Just the radiator ticking through a fragile kind of peace.
My shoes were by the doorway, already soaked through and worn,
And my socks gave up their winter like a memory reborn.
Everything was waiting, though nothing said a word—
Just the sound of something ending that nobody had heard.

She sat beside the window in the chair she wouldn’t leave,
A blanket over folded hands that no longer seemed to grieve.
A cup of tea beside her, gone dull upon the top,
Like time had touched it gently and forgotten how to stop.
“Is it still raining?” she asked, though she was looking through the glass,
And I said, “It’s been raining since the night turned into last.”

And the rain keeps its own time,
Washing everything clean and leaving nothing behind.
It taps out all the answers we were never meant to find,
Soft and steady, crossing every line.
And I stood there in the quiet, where the world had come undone,
Knowing some distances are not the kind you can run.

I knelt beside her slowly, like I might still be seen,
Like recognition could return the way it used to mean.
But her eyes moved through my face like a room she’d never known,
Searching for a reason in a language she’d outgrown.
Then she spoke so softly it was almost not a sound—
“Are you here to fix the window?” though none was broken around.

And something shifted then, not breaking, not quite pain—
Just the air becoming certain, like it understood the rain.
I could stay there in the moment, let the hours pass us by,
But I couldn’t reach the place she was already saying goodbye.
Not with words, not with wanting, not with anything I knew—
Some doors don’t open backward, no matter what you do.

Final Chorus
And the rain keeps its own time,
Running down the glass like it’s erasing every line.
It washes over everything we thought we could define,
Soft and steady, leaving none of it behind.
And I stayed there in the quiet, where nothing could be won,
Just listening to the rain say what couldn’t be undone.

songs.scenario: watching the old house get sold with a teacher who believed in me, out on the prairie
songs.lyrics:
The wind was writing on the tall dry grass,
When we stood where the old house used to pass
Into something new with a “sold” on the door,
Like a story that lives there anymore.
Paint peeling like it couldn’t hold on tight,
To all we learned in that fading light.

You stood beside me, quiet and sure,
A teacher who made the small things feel pure.
Said, “Nothing you love really disappears,
It just changes shape across the years.”

Out on the prairie where the sky runs wide,
We watched the past get pulled inside
A stranger’s keys, a closing gate,
A life we knew we couldn’t save.
But you said, “Look closer, don’t turn away—
Even endings have something to say.”

The floorboards remembered every step we took,
Every borrowed dream, every second look.
I saw my younger self in the dust-filled glass,
Wondering how something so fast could pass.

You talked of algebra and storms that break,
Of how even silence has what it takes
To teach you the shape of letting go,
And the kind of strength you only grow.

Out on the prairie where the sky runs wide,
We watched the past get pulled inside
A stranger’s keys, a closing gate,
A life we knew we couldn’t save.
But you said, “Don’t let it turn to pain—
Some things leave so something remains.”

And I swear the wind called both our names,
Like chalk dust rising from forgotten names,
From classrooms gone and fields of gold,
From all the things we’re told we hold.

Now I carry that day like a folded map,
Where the house and the lesson both collapse
Into one clear truth I didn’t see before:
You can lose a place and still learn more.

Final Chorus
Out on the prairie where the sky runs wide,
I let the old house fall inside
Not as loss, but a turning page,
A quiet kind of coming of age.
And your voice still follows the drifting grain:
“Even endings can teach you something again.”

songs.scenario: the night I finally left home with a neighbor I grew up next to, out on the prairie
songs.lyrics:
We slipped out through the kitchen light,
Where the kettle hummed and the porch lamp sighed,
Your boots were waiting by the garden gate,
Like they’d always known the time was right.

The fence was older than both our names,
Bent like a secret it never gave away,
You looked at me like you’d rehearsed this line
Since we were kids in the tall grass days.

And the prairie opened like a held breath,
Endless and honest and wide as fear,
No map, no promise, no looking back—
Just the wind saying you’re already here.

We left home in the quiet way,
No slammed doors, no last goodbye,
Just two shadows learning how to move
Where the horizon meets the sky.

The stars came out like they knew our plan,
Like they’d been waiting for us to begin,
You said, “We don’t have to be anyone yet,”
And I finally let that truth sink in.

The road was only a ribbon of dust,
But it felt like a line we could finally cross,
Every mile a shedding of what we were,
Every mile a gain and a loss.

We left home in the quiet way,
With borrowed courage and trembling hands,
Two kids from a map that never changed
Stepping off it into open land.

Now when I dream, I still see that night,
Your silhouette in the prairie glow,
And I wonder if leaving was running away,
Or the first time I chose to go.

songs.scenario: the morning I knew it was over with the person who raised me, in a cramped city apartment
songs.story:
The morning I knew it was over, the kettle had already boiled dry.

I remember the sound first—that thin, panicked clicking of metal expanding in an empty pot. It echoed in our cramped apartment like something knocking from the inside. The kitchen window was fogged from last night’s heat, and the condensation had dragged slow rivers down the glass, cutting the city outside into warped lines of gray brick and passing coats.

They were at the table already, sitting in the same chair they always claimed by habit, though nothing about our lives felt habitual anymore. A mug sat in front of them, tea gone cold, a skin forming across the surface like it had given up waiting to be drunk. Their hands were wrapped around it anyway, not for warmth, but like it was something to hold onto so they wouldn’t drift away.

I stood there barefoot on the tile. It was colder than it should’ve been. The radiator had been making that uneven rattling sound all night, like it was chewing on something it couldn’t swallow.

“Did you sleep?” I asked, though I already knew the answer from the hollow shape of their face.

They shrugged. Not unkindly. Just… emptied. Then they looked at me for a second too long, like they were trying to remember who I had been before I became someone they had to disappoint.

That was the moment it shifted, though nothing dramatic happened. No shouting. No breaking. Just a small, almost polite realization settling into the space between us: there was nothing left to repair that we both still believed in.

I turned to the sink to run water over the kettle, because I didn’t know what else to do with my hands. The pipes coughed before giving anything steady, and the smell of iron and old metal rose up, sharp enough to sting my nose. Behind me, a chair scraped—slow, deliberate. They stood, not rushing, not delaying. Just finally done pretending the morning might turn into something else.

When I looked back, they had already put on their coat.

It was too thin for the weather. I remember thinking that with an absurd clarity, like it mattered more than anything else. The hallway light flickered above them, buzzing faintly, catching in the gray of their hair.

“I should go,” they said.

And I didn’t ask them to stay.

After the door closed, the apartment felt smaller than before, like it had exhaled and never bothered to inhale again. I poured the kettle out into the sink. The water ran clear, then cold, then gone.

Only then did I understand: it wasn’t the leaving that ended us.

It was the quiet we both kept choosing, right up until the morning it finally spoke louder than either of us could.
songs.lyrics:
The kettle cried in metal clicks that morning,
an empty song in a crowded little room.
Steam had long since left without a warning,
just fogged glass and the shape of too much gloom.
The city stretched like bruised and tired motion,
through rivers drawn by condensation’s hand,
and you were there like someone held in slow erosion,
still trying to stay somewhere neither of us planned.

The radiator coughed its broken rhythm,
like it forgot the words it used to know.
And everything between us sat so still then,
like even silence didn’t want to go.

It wasn’t loud, it wasn’t breaking,
no final line, no door slammed shut.
Just two hearts quietly unmaking
what neither of us could call enough.
And I didn’t ask you to stay that morning,
though I felt it in my chest too late—
some things don’t end with warning,
they end the way we learn to wait.

Your hands around a mug gone cold and fading,
like warmth had left and didn’t say goodbye.
You held it like a memory you were bracing,
so it wouldn’t fall apart or ask you why.
You looked at me like I was someone distant,
a life you used to know but couldn’t name.
And I stood there, quietly resistant
to saying what would only sound like blame.

The pipes beneath the sink kept on complaining,
like they knew more than we ever would admit.
And every drop of nothing left remaining
felt heavier than anything we’d quit.

It wasn’t loud, it wasn’t breaking,
no final line, no door slammed shut.
Just two hearts quietly unmaking
what neither of us could call enough.
And I didn’t ask you to stay that morning,
though I felt it in my chest too late—
some things don’t end with warning,
they end the way we learn to wait.

You said, “I should go,” like it was weather,
like it was something neither of us chose.
And I just watched you pull yourself together,
in a coat too thin for whatever comes and goes.
The hallway light flickered like it doubted
every version of us it used to see—
and I think that’s when I finally understood it:
we were already gone, quietly.

Final Chorus
It wasn’t loud, it wasn’t breaking,
just a silence we kept letting grow.
Two almosts slowly overtaking
the life we no longer could hold.
And I didn’t ask you to stay that morning,
and you didn’t turn around to say—
we ended long before the warning,
we just arrived there anyway.

The kettle’s cold. The room is smaller.
The city keeps on moving past the pane.
And I pour out what’s left of morning water,
like it might wash away what can’t remain.

songs.scenario: watching the old house get sold with a teacher who believed in me, in a small rust-belt town
songs.lyrics:
(A blues song)

The yard got quiet where the laughter used to climb,
Boarded-up swing set, rusted over time.
That old house leaning like it’s tired of standing tall,
I watched a man with a pen decide it all.
Paper in his hand like a verdict in the wind,
Signed away the corners where my childhood’s been.
And I stood there helpless as the shutters shut their eyes,
Like the whole damn past just learned how to say goodbye.

Oh Lord, it’s a slow, low moan when the old place goes,
Like a train you hear but you never quite know.
And the heart keeps beatin’ but it don’t feel right,
When the light goes out on a rust-belt night.
Yeah, I’m standin’ in the ashes of what used to be,
Watchin’ my old house get sold right in front of me.

Down at the schoolhouse, chalk dust in the air,
She saw somethin’ in me I didn’t know was there.
Said, “Boy, you got fire, don’t let it go cold,”
When everything around us was turnin’ to mold.
While the mills shut down and the streets went thin,
She taught me how to stand up when the world caves in.
Now I hear her voice like a gospel line,
Saying, “Don’t you let that town steal what’s mine.”

Oh Lord, it’s a slow, low moan when the old place goes,
Like a train you hear but you never quite know.
And the heart keeps beatin’ but it don’t feel right,
When the light goes out on a rust-belt night.
Yeah, I’m standin’ in the ashes of what used to be,
Watchin’ my old house get sold right in front of me.

That teacher said, “Memory ain’t just what you lose,
It’s the road you walk when you don’t get to choose.”
So I carry these streets like a worn-out tune,
Under broken streetlights and a bruised-up moon.
And maybe the walls fall, maybe the past is gone,
But the words she gave me keep me holdin’ on.

Final Chorus
Oh Lord, it’s a slow, low moan when the old place goes,
But there’s something in me that still somehow knows—
That even when the factories close and die,
A kid can still learn how to touch the sky.
Yeah, I’m standin’ in the rubble, but I can still see,
More than just a house that got sold on me.

So here’s to the teacher who saw through the dust,
And the bones of a town that forgot how to trust…
And here’s to the silence where the old floors creak,
Still whisperin’ secrets every time I speak.

songs.scenario: the fight that ended it with a teacher who believed in me, in a cramped city apartment
songs.story:
The radiator in my apartment never stopped ticking, even in summer. That night it sounded louder, like it was trying to interrupt us.

She stood near my kitchen counter—too close for the space, too close for the conversation. My whole apartment was one room pretending to be three: bed behind a hanging sheet, stove pressed against the sink, window that looked straight into another building’s brick skin. I remember the smell of instant coffee I’d left half-finished and the wet wool of my coat on the chair.

“You’re wasting it,” she said quietly.

That was worse than shouting.

She had been my teacher once, back when I still wrote things in notebooks instead of deleting them at 2 a.m. She was the one who told me I had “a voice that doesn’t apologize.” I used to repeat that line like a prayer. After I graduated, she kept checking in—first emails, then coffee, then this strange blur where mentorship turned into something I never named out loud.

Now she was looking at my latest draft on my laptop, screen light cutting her face into hard angles.

“It’s careful,” she said. “You’re hiding again.”

I laughed because I didn’t know what else to do. “Or maybe I’m just not as good as you thought.”

That was the spark. Her chair scraped the floor when she stood straighter.

“No,” she said. “Don’t do that. Don’t make me the excuse.”

The room felt suddenly smaller, like the walls had leaned in to listen. I could hear someone upstairs walking too heavily, pipes groaning, the distant siren that always made the city feel like it was remembering something it didn’t want to.

I told her she didn’t get to follow me out of school and still grade me. She told me I was throwing away everything she tried to build in me. My voice got sharper than I meant it to, words knocking into the sink, the counter, the edge of the bed. At some point I was pointing at the laptop like it had betrayed me.

Then she said it—soft, almost sad: “I believed in you more than you do.”

That’s when something in me cracked instead of snapped. I went silent. The radiator kept ticking.

I don’t remember deciding to end it. I only remember opening the door. Cold hallway air rushed in, carrying the smell of fried onions from a neighbor’s dinner. She didn’t follow right away.

Before she left, she placed my printed pages on the counter, straightened them like she used to in class.

“You don’t need me to tell you who you are,” she said.

Then she was gone, footsteps fading down the stairwell.

I sat on the floor for a long time, my back against the bed frame, listening to the apartment settle again into itself. Only later did I realize the silence wasn’t empty. It was waiting.
songs.lyrics:
The radiator never learned to stop talking,
even in July it was counting the air.
Tick—like a metronome stuck in the wall,
like something insisting that someone was there.

Your coat still hung heavy with yesterday’s weather,
coffee gone cold in a half-used cup,
and the room I called a room was just borders and corners,
trying its best to add up.

You stood too close for the shape of the kitchen,
too close for the words we both knew were near,
and the light from my laptop cut you into silence—
all sharp lines and things I won’t hear.

And I laughed like a door that won’t close right,
like I didn’t feel the hinge giving way,
you said I was hiding, I said I was honest,
and neither of us knew what to say.

Don’t make me the reason you break what you built,
don’t make me the proof that you’re lost,
I believed in you more than you do—
and that belief comes at a cost.
The walls leaned in, the pipes all sighed,
the city remembered our names,
and the radiator kept time in the silence
like it knew how this ends the same.

We traded our lines like they were forgiveness,
each sentence a spark on dry floorboards,
you pointed at pages like they were a verdict,
like they could undo what we were before words.

The hallway was colder than anything spoken,
fried onions drifting up through the stair,
and I thought about all the versions of us
that never quite made it upstairs.

You opened the door like you’d opened a chapter
you couldn’t afford to keep reading through,
and the air came in like a truth uninvited—
too sharp, too clean, too new.

And I didn’t follow,
not because I didn’t care,
but because some endings don’t move when you call them,
they just wait in the stairwell air.

I straightened your pages like old classroom habits,
like order could soften the blow,
and I said what I meant, or what meant something like it—
then I let myself go.

Final Chorus
Don’t make me the reason you break what you built,
don’t make me the line you erase,
I believed in you more than you do—
but I can’t stay in that place.
The radiator ticks like it knows what we left,
like it measures the time we lost,
and silence is never really empty—
it’s just what remains after cost.

Now the room is just room again,
no leaning, no arguing sound,
only the patient old radiator
keeping everything grounded.

songs.scenario: the last real conversation we had with the friend I lost touch with, in a town that's barely there anymore
songs.story:
I still remember the way the town sounded when it was half-awake.

Not fully alive, not fully gone—somewhere in between, like it was always deciding whether to stay. The train stopped there only twice a day, and even then it sounded like it was apologizing for the interruption.

We met at the old station bench, the one with the cracked green paint that never got replaced because, as the mayor used to say, “It still holds.” You showed up late, like always, hands shoved into your jacket pockets, breath fogging in the cold air that smelled faintly of iron and wet wood.

“You look like you haven’t slept,” I said.

“You look like you’ve been trying too hard to leave,” you replied, half-smiling.

That was the thing about you—you never softened the truth just to make it easier to swallow.

We walked down Main Street, though calling it that now feels generous. Half the storefronts were boarded up, the bakery sign missing its “r,” so it just read “BAKE Y,” like a joke nobody had bothered to finish. Snowmelt dripped from the gutters in slow, deliberate drops. Every step echoed more than it should have.

We ended up by the river, where the bridge used to be before they decided it wasn’t worth repairing. Only the concrete stumps remained, sticking out of the water like broken teeth.

That’s where the conversation turned.

You said, “I got offered a job in the city. I leave next week.”

I remember laughing, thinking it was one of your half-jokes. “You’ve said that before.”

But you didn’t laugh back.
