Like the world outside never trusted us,
And the sky leans low like it’s listening in
To the places you end and the ones you begin.

We’re driving through a town that forgot its name,
Where every goodbye looks just the same,
And I don’t know what you’re trying to save—
But I won’t let you go through it alone in this place.

songs.scenario: the morning I knew it was over with a neighbor I grew up next to, in a cramped city apartment
songs.lyrics:
Morning light on a cracked kitchen tile,
Kettle humming like it always does for a while.
Same thin walls, same hallway sigh,
But something in the air won’t let me lie.

I heard footsteps in the room next door,
Like they meant something different than before.
We grew up here, side by side,
Now there’s a silence neither one can hide.

And I don’t know when it changed its name,
From “us” to something I can’t claim…

That morning I knew it was over,
Not with a crash, not a fight, not a storm.
Just a quiet I couldn’t get closer,
Like the shape of a home that’s no longer warm.
In a cramped city where nothing moves on,
Except the feeling that something is gone.
Yeah, that morning I knew it was over—
And we were still living next door.

Stairwell echoes, familiar sound,
Every step like it’s falling down.
I caught your name in the elevator air,
Like a habit we forgot was there.

We shared the same thin winter heat,
The same small view of the crowded street.
But even walls can learn to divide
What used to feel like the same inside.

And I kept thinking it would pass,
But some things don’t come back…

That morning I knew it was over,
Not with a door slam, just distance instead.
Like the space between what we never said,
Growing louder than anything said.
In a building that remembers our past,
But won’t let the feeling last.
Yeah, that morning I knew it was over—
And we were still living next door.

No final goodbye, no clean release,
Just two old stories losing peace.
And I wonder if you felt it too,
That quiet undoing between me and you.

Final Chorus
That morning I knew it was over,
Like sunlight that didn’t feel the same.
Like a friendship we never got closure,
Just a hallway that stopped calling my name.
In a city that never lets go,
But forgets what it used to know.
Yeah, that morning I knew it was over—
And I heard it in everything more.

Same thin walls, same morning light,
But nothing in them feels quite right.

songs.scenario: the night I finally left home with the person who raised me, in a cramped city apartment
songs.story:
The night we left, the apartment felt smaller than it ever had—like the walls had been quietly inching inward for years and finally decided to stop pretending otherwise.

It was one room pretending to be three if you stood in the right places. The radiator knocked in uneven bursts, ticking like a restless clock. Outside, the city never really slept; sirens drifted up through the single cracked window, mixing with the smell of damp concrete and the fried onion grease that lived permanently in the stairwell.

We packed without talking much. There wasn’t much left to say that hadn’t already been said in glances over years of shared silence. My hands kept brushing the same things: the chipped mug with the faded blue rim, the stack of unopened letters we both agreed we didn’t need to read, the photograph stuck to the fridge by a magnet shaped like a sunflower. The fridge hummed, stubbornly alive, like it didn’t understand we were about to abandon it.

They—who raised me, who learned how to be a parent in the same way you learn a language by being dropped inside it—folded clothes with slow precision. Every fold looked like an apology they couldn’t say out loud. At one point, they paused with a sweater in their hands and just held it there, suspended, as if remembering the body that used to fill it more than the fabric itself.

The turning point came when we ran out of boxes.

It was so small, so stupidly ordinary. I remember laughing once, sharp and sudden, because there were still too many things left and nowhere left to put them. That laugh broke something open. They looked at me then—not as the child they once carried through illness, or school mornings, or heartbreaks—but as someone standing on the edge of leaving them behind in a different way.

“I don’t know how to do this part,” they said quietly.

Not the moving. Not the lifting or the logistics. The leaving.

The air between us tightened. I realized we weren’t just packing a life; we were dividing one. And neither of us had been given instructions for that.

So we stopped.

We sat on the floor because there was nowhere else to sit. The carpet was worn thin in the middle, flattened by years of pacing and waiting. I could feel the chill of it through my jeans. Outside, rain started—light at first, then steady, tapping against the glass like a hesitant knock.

They reached into a pocket and pulled out a single key. Old brass, dulled at the edges. It was the last one. They pressed it into my palm without looking at me.

“For you,” they said. “So it doesn’t feel like it disappears.”

It was absurd. We were both leaving. There was nowhere for it to unlock anymore.

But I closed my fingers around it anyway, feeling the weight of it—small, cold, undeniable.

When we finally stood up again, the apartment didn’t feel like it was shrinking anymore. It just felt empty, like it had been holding its breath and finally let go.

We turned off the light. And for a second in the dark, before the door clicked sh
songs.lyrics:
The walls were closer than we ever admitted,
Like they’d been listening in for years.
One room split into three if you stood just right,
With silence louder than our fears.
The radiator coughed its tired confession,
A clock that forgot how to behave,
And the city hummed through a cracked window frame
Like it never learned how to behave.

We moved like ghosts in familiar clothing,
Hands brushing things we didn’t name—
A chipped blue mug, unopened letters,
And a fridge that still called us the same.

No words left worth repeating,
Just glances worn too thin,
Like every conversation
We never quite stepped in.

So we packed up a life in pieces and quiet,
Where everything mattered and nothing would fit,
And I laughed once, too sharp to hold it,
At the absurdity of it.
You looked at me like a door left open
To a room we were both walking through,
And said, “I don’t know how to do this part,”
Like there were instructions for leaving you.

You folded sweaters like apologies,
Slow hands trying not to break.
Each crease a kind of memory
You weren’t ready to remake.
And I saw it then—not just a parent,
But someone learning love from the start,
Dropping every rule they were never given
Into the shape of a breaking heart.

We ran out of boxes like it meant something,
Like the universe had a point to prove.
And suddenly the room was too honest to stay in,
Too small for what we couldn’t move.

The air got tight between us,
Like it knew what we’d become,
Two people holding endings
That had already begun.

So we packed up a life in pieces and quiet,
What stayed, what left, what we couldn’t save,
And you pressed into my palm a single old key
Like it could keep anything from change.
It was heavy with doors that didn’t exist anymore,
Still warm from somewhere we knew,
And I held it like a promise we both understood
Even if neither of us knew.

We sat on the floor where the years had thinned it,
Listening to rain start slow, then sure,
Like it had been waiting outside our story
For a chance to become the cure.
And nothing was fixed, and nothing was finished,
Just held in the middle of “then,”
Where leaving isn’t an ending at all—
Just a way we begin again.

Final Chorus
So we turned off the light in a room full of echoes,
Let the dark do what it always knew,
And for one suspended breath in the doorway,
There was no me, no you.
Just the weight of a key in a closing hand,
And a house that finally exhaled,
As if it had been holding us gently forever
Until the moment we failed.

The door clicked shut like a sentence ending,
No period, just air in between—
And somewhere behind us, the apartment kept listening
To the life it had already seen.

songs.scenario: the last real conversation we had with the person who raised me, by the coast in late autumn
songs.story:
The wind coming off the coast had that late-autumn bite, the kind that gets into your sleeves no matter how tight you pull your coat. The sea wasn’t dramatic that day—no crashing theater, just a steady, tired breathing against the rocks below the path. Gray on gray, sky and water stitched together so cleanly it was hard to tell where one ended.

We stood there longer than we needed to, both of us pretending the view was the reason.

They kept their hands in their pockets. I remember noticing the frayed edge of their cuff, the same one I used to tug on as a kid when I didn’t want them to leave a room. Back then, that sleeve meant safety. That day, it just looked worn.

“You’ve been quiet,” they said finally, not looking at me.

“I don’t know what there is left to say,” I answered, though that wasn’t entirely true. There was too much to say, and none of it arranged itself into anything usable.

A gull cut across the waterline, low and effortless. The sound of it felt louder than our voices.

They nodded like they understood, which somehow made it worse.

“I did what I thought I had to,” they said after a while. The sentence landed between us without urgency, like it had been rehearsed on other walks, in other weather.

I wanted to argue. I wanted to list every small absence, every moment I learned to read silence instead of comfort. But when I opened my mouth, what came out was smaller.

“You were there,” I said. “Just not always… with me.”

That was the turning point, though I didn’t recognize it immediately. They flinched—not dramatically, just a slight shift in posture, like the wind had changed direction inside them.

For the first time, they turned fully toward me. Their face looked older in a way that wasn’t about wrinkles. It was the recognition of something they couldn’t redo.

“I thought presence was enough,” they said quietly.

The tide pulled back, exposing wet stone that immediately began to darken again. I remember focusing on that, because it was easier than holding their eyes.

When we started walking back toward the car, neither of us fixed it. There was no resolution, no sudden forgiveness. Just the sound of gravel underfoot and the distant, indifferent sea continuing its work behind us.

But I remember, as we reached the steps, they slowed down and let their shoulder brush mine—not accidental, not quite deliberate either. And I didn’t move away.
songs.lyrics:
The wind came in off the water like it knew our names,
Crawled through the sleeves of a coat that never kept it tame.
Sky and sea were stitched together, no line to be found,
Just a quiet kind of ending where the light ran down.

We stood like the view had asked us both to stay,
But it wasn’t the horizon we were trying to say.
Your hands stayed buried where they always learned to hide,
And I watched the past hang loose from your cuff’s frayed side.

I used to pull that sleeve when I was small,
Like it could stop you from ever leaving at all…

And I don’t know when we became strangers in the same air,
Talking in pieces like nobody’s really there.
You said you did what you had to, like that explains the rain,
But presence isn’t shelter when it doesn’t ease the pain.
Still we stood there, where the sea meets stone and sky,
Too much to say, and no clean way to try.

A gull cut low across the waterline and gone,
Like something effortless pretending nothing’s wrong.
You spoke like you’d rehearsed it on a thousand quiet walks,
Like guilt could be translated into careful talks.

“I thought presence was enough,” you said, almost low,
And I watched the tide undo the shore I used to know.
Wet stone reappearing just to darken once again,
Like memory refusing to pretend.

I looked at the ground because it hurt less than your face,
And silence filled every empty space.

And I don’t know when we became strangers in the same air,
Talking in pieces like nobody’s really there.
You said you did what you had to, like that explains the rain,
But presence isn’t shelter when it doesn’t ease the pain.
Still we stood there, where the sea meets stone and sky,
Too much to say, and no clean way to try.

No resolution falling from the clouds that day,
No forgiveness arriving to rewrite what we’d made.
Just gravel underfoot and the coast’s indifferent sound,
And everything unspoken still hanging around.

On the way back down the path, nothing got repaired,
No grand confession, no “I wish I’d been there.”
But at the steps you slowed, like something almost broke through,
And your shoulder brushed mine like you didn’t mean it to.

I didn’t move away.
I just let it stay.

Final Chorus
And maybe that’s how we became honest in the air,
Not in the answers, just in standing there.
You said you did what you had to, and I let it be true,
While the sea kept doing what it’s always gonna do.
Still we stood there, where the past won’t say goodbye,
Under a gray on gray on gray kind of sky.

songs.scenario: the fight that ended it with someone I met once on a train, out on the prairie
songs.story:
I only met him once, but the sound of that train still lives in my ribs like it never left.

It was late afternoon when I got on—one of those long, thin routes cutting through open prairie, where the sky feels too big for the land holding it. The carriage smelled like old vinyl seats and metal dust baked warm by the sun. He was already there, sitting across the aisle, boots planted wide like he belonged more to the ground than the train.

We didn’t talk at first. Just the rhythm of wheels, the soft clatter like teeth chattering in a cold room. Outside, fields rolled by in waves of yellow grass and wire fences that never seemed to end. Then he said something—small, almost careless. I don’t even remember the words, just the way he looked at me after, like he’d already decided I wouldn’t answer.

But I did.

We started talking the way strangers do when they think they’ll forget each other in an hour. Weather. Routes. Why anyone stays in places like this. His voice had a rough edge, like gravel dragged under a tire. Mine felt too soft for the space between us.

At some point the conversation tilted. I said something about leaving things behind. He laughed—not kindly. Said leaving is just another way of lying to yourself. I told him he didn’t know me. That was the first crack.

By the time the train slowed near an unmanned stop—just a rusted sign and wind pushing dust in slow spirals—we were no longer pretending.

He stood first. I remember the way the light caught the window behind him, turning him into a cut-out shape. “People like you always think distance fixes things,” he said. I told him people like him always think staying makes them right.

It wasn’t shouting. It was worse—quiet, precise, like placing stones on a grave.

We stepped off without meaning to. No station, just gravel crunching underfoot and the train exhaling behind us as it waited. The prairie swallowed sound fast out there. Even our breathing felt exposed.

The fight wasn’t loud. It was a tightening, a stripping away. Words sharpened until there was nothing soft left to hold them. At some point I realized we weren’t arguing about travel or staying or leaving. We were arguing about whether either of us had ever been forgiven for anything.

The turning point came when he said my name. Not how I’d told him, but the way someone says it when they’re trying to keep you from walking away. And I stopped.

Because I realized he was right about one thing: I was already gone. I had been since I stepped onto that train, since I agreed to sit across from a stranger and pretend it might mean nothing.

The train started moving again behind us, slow at first, then faster, until it was just a thread of sound pulling itself out of the horizon.

Neither of us chased it.
songs.lyrics:
“Prairie Train (What We Didn’t Say)”

I got on late when the light was bending low,
long thin line where the wild grasses grow.
Carriage humming like a secret in the heat,
old vinyl seats and dust on tired feet.
He was there like he’d always been there first,
boots wide like the ground had quenched his thirst.
We didn’t speak, just the wheels confessing time,
metal teeth in a slow, rhythmic rhyme.

And I still hear that train inside my ribs at night,
like it never left, like it’s still in flight.
Two strangers talking like the world won’t stay,
on a line that drags the sky away.
We didn’t know what we were trying to prove—
only that neither of us could move.

He said something small, I don’t recall the sound,
just the way his eyes cut and then held me down.
Like he’d already written what I’d deny,
like I was a story he’d already tried.
We spoke in weather, in roads that don’t return,
in the kind of silence that makes the inside burn.
My voice too soft for the space between,
his like gravel under engines unseen.

I said leaving things is how I stay intact,
he laughed like truth is something you can’t take back.
Said, “You don’t leave, you just rename the chain,”
I told him he didn’t know my name.
That was the crack where the quiet slipped through,
where neither of us knew what to do.
We weren’t talking about rails or land—
just forgiveness we couldn’t understand.

And I still hear that train inside my ribs at night,
like it never left, like it’s still in flight.
Two strangers talking like the world won’t stay,
on a line that drags the sky away.
We didn’t know what we were trying to prove—
only that neither of us could move.

At the unmanned stop where the dust won’t settle right,
he stood in the window cut from fading light.
Said, “People like you think distance heals the bone,”
I said, “People like you think staying makes a home.”
Not shouting—just the careful placement of stones,
on graves we hadn’t yet known.

We stepped down like it wasn’t a choice we made,
just gravel and wind and the price we paid.
The prairie swallowed every sound we broke,
even breathing felt like something we spoke.
He said my name like a hand on my sleeve,
like asking permission not to leave.

But I was already gone before the door,
before the tracks, before the war.
Gone like a ticket with no return,
like a lesson neither of us would learn.

Final Chorus
And I still hear that train inside my ribs at night,
slipping away into the fading light.
Two strangers standing where the endings stay,
watching everything they didn’t say.
It didn’t matter who was wrong or right—
only that the train moved on without us that night.

songs.scenario: the summer everything changed with my childhood best friend, out on the prairie
songs.story:
The summer everything changed, the prairie felt like it had no edges.

We were thirteen, and Jonas and I had mapped every inch of it like it belonged to us. The grass came up to our waists in the low spots, rippling gold and green under a wind that never seemed to stop moving. When it was hot, it smelled like dust baked into sweetness—dry earth, crushed stems, and the faint iron bite of the old fence posts that ran along the field behind his house.

We spent whole days out there. No phones, no clocks. Just the sun climbing and falling like it had nowhere else to be.

There was a storm that summer we still talked about before we stopped talking at all. It started as a smear of gray on the horizon, soft at first, like a bruise forming in the sky. Jonas said we should go back. I said it would pass. It always did.

We were wrong about that.

The wind changed direction so fast it felt like the land itself had turned its head. The grass flattened in waves, and the air went sharp with electricity and dirt. Somewhere far off, something cracked—maybe a tree, maybe just the sky deciding to split open. We ran toward the old irrigation ditch, laughing at first, half terrified, half thrilled, rain already slapping our faces like thrown gravel.

That’s when Jonas slipped.

It wasn’t dramatic. That was the worst part. Just one step gone wrong on wet clay, his boot sliding out from under him, his arm grabbing mine hard enough to leave a bruise. For a second I held him there, leaning back, feet digging in, feeling his weight and the storm and everything pulling at once.

“Don’t let go,” he said.

And I didn’t—until I did.

I still don’t know why. The mud was too slick, the rain too loud, my grip too small for what it was holding. My hand opened like it had made its own decision.

He went down the embankment into the dark water of the ditch.

I remember the sound after: just rain. No shouting. No wind. Like the world had agreed to pretend nothing had happened.

When he came up, he wasn’t looking at me. He crawled out, stood, and walked past me toward the fields without saying a word. I followed for a while, but the prairie was suddenly too wide, and I couldn’t tell if he was getting farther away or if I was just stopping.

That was the summer everything changed.

After that, the grass still grew the same color. The wind still came every afternoon. But it never felt like it belonged to us again.
songs.lyrics:
We were thirteen in a kingdom of green,
where the prairie had no ending line, no seam.
Mapped it all like it was ours to keep,
golden grass up to our knees, up to our dreams.
No clocks, no calls, just the sun overhead,
and the hum of the wind like something unsaid.
Fence posts rusted with an iron taste,
and time moved slow in that endless place.

We thought the sky would always behave,
we thought storms were just something that passed away.

But that was the summer everything changed,
when the wind turned sharp and the sky got strange.
And I can still feel your hand in mine,
slipping through the moment I crossed the line.
You said, “Don’t let go,” and I tried to stay,
but the earth turned slick and I lost my way.
Now every blade of grass still calls your name—
that was the summer everything changed.

It started like a bruise in the distant blue,
soft at first like it wasn’t meant for you.
Jonas said “we should go,” I said “not yet,”
like the world had ever broken a promise before it kept.
Then the wind stood up and the prairie bowed,
like even the ground was backing down.
We ran through lightning in the heavy air,
laughing like fear didn’t live there.

The ditch was waiting like a cut in the land,
rain hitting harder than we could stand.
One wrong step and the world tilted fast,
and I felt your weight pull me into the past.
I held on like holding could change the fall,
like I was bigger than gravity, bigger than it all.
But my hand opened—don’t ask me why—
like it belonged to someone else in the sky.

You went down where the water ran black,
and the storm went quiet and never looked back.
No shout, no break, no final sound,
just rain like it was washing the whole thing down.

That was the summer everything changed,
when the wind turned sharp and the sky got strange.
And I can still feel your hand in mine,
like a ghost in the mud at the edge of time.
You said, “Don’t let go,” and I tried to stay,
but the world doesn’t bend when it wants to break.
Now every field feels a little bit wrong—
like something’s missing in the same old song.

The grass still grows the same shade of gold,
the wind still moves like it always was told.
But I don’t walk there like I used to then—
because nothing belongs to us again.

songs.scenario: moving to a new city completely alone with my estranged father, on a cross-country highway
songs.story:
I didn’t think silence could have weight until I spent twelve hours in a sedan with my father, driving west across a country that didn’t feel like mine anymore.

He insisted on driving. Said it was “simpler that way,” like simplicity was something you could pack in the trunk beside two duffel bags and a box of dishes that still smelled faintly like my old apartment. I sat in the passenger seat, knees angled toward the glove compartment, watching the highway unspool like a ribbon we were both afraid to touch.

He didn’t talk much. Neither did I. The air conditioner rattled whenever we went uphill, and the smell of old upholstery mixed with burnt coffee from the gas station cup he kept refilling without ever finishing. Every few hours we’d stop—always the same routine. Fuel, restroom, silence again.

I kept noticing small things about him I didn’t remember from childhood. The way his hands tightened on the wheel when trucks passed too close. The faint tremor in his jaw when he thought I wasn’t looking. At one stop, I saw him standing under the flickering lights of a roadside station, staring at a vending machine like it might explain something to him. He looked older than I remembered. Or maybe I just hadn’t been looking before.

We hadn’t spoken properly in three years. Not since the argument that neither of us ever finished. Now we were sharing an engine and a destination: a small apartment in a city neither of us had chosen, where I was supposed to start over and he was supposed to “help me settle,” as if help wasn’t something you had to learn how to accept.

Somewhere past the halfway point, the sky went the color of bruised metal. Rain started as a thin static against the windshield, then thickened until the wipers couldn’t quite keep up. He slowed the car. The road blurred into a smear of headlights and water.

“That junction back there,” he said suddenly, voice rough like it had been unused for a while, “I took a wrong turn there once. Years ago. Didn’t tell anyone for a long time.”

I didn’t answer at first. Then: “Did it matter?”

He exhaled, almost a laugh. “I think it did. I think that’s why I remember it.”

The rain grew heavier. For a moment the world narrowed to the rhythm of the wipers—left, right, left, right—like something trying to keep time for us when we couldn’t.

I realized then that we weren’t just driving to a new city. We were passing through everything we had never said. And for the first time since we left, I stopped pretending I didn’t know him at all.

When I finally looked over, he was already watching me, like he’d been waiting for me to notice.
songs.lyrics:
Twelve hours in a sedan with the past between us,
highway like a question neither one of us trusts.
You said, “I’ll drive, it’s simpler this way,”
like simple was something you could pack and take away.
Old box of dishes, apartment in the trunk,
coffee gone cold but the habit still clung.
And I watched the world turn into blurred glass lines,
while we both sat quiet, out of borrowed time.

Every exit looked like a way we could’ve turned,
every mile a lesson neither of us learned.
And the silence wasn’t empty—it was full and loud,
like a storm sitting low inside a cloud.

Left, right, left—keep the wheels in motion,
two hearts drifting on the same broken ocean.
We don’t speak, but it still cuts through,
every unsaid thing in the space between me and you.
And I didn’t know a silence could weigh so much
until it filled this car, until it filled our touch.
Left, right, left—down a road we don’t name,
but I think I finally know you again the same.

Gas station lights like tired little suns,
you stood there staring like you’d already run.
Hands on the wheel, knuckles pulling tight,
every truck passing just a little too bright.
I saw the tremor you tried to hide in your jaw,
a man rewriting everything he thought he saw.
And I wondered when you stopped being just “dad,”
and started being someone I never really had.

And the windshield bled with a gray kind of rain,
like the sky was trying to translate our pain.
Wipers kept time like a metronome plea,
left, right—don’t forget how to breathe.

Left, right, left—keep the wheels in motion,
two hearts drifting on the same broken ocean.
We don’t speak, but it still cuts through,
every unsaid thing in the space between me and you.
And I didn’t know a silence could weigh so much
until it filled this car, until it filled our touch.
Left, right, left—down a road we don’t name,
but I think I finally know you again the same.

You said you missed a turn years ago back there,
like regret can still be parked somewhere.
And I almost said, “Does it matter now?”
but the answer was hanging in the storm somehow.
So I just stayed there, letting it land,
watching age in the grip of your hand.

Final Chorus
Left, right, left—through the rain and the distance,
through the years we survived by pure resistance.
We don’t fix it, but something breaks through,
in the quiet that finally tells the truth.
And I didn’t know a silence could turn to sound
until it pulled us back from falling down.
Left, right, left—still nowhere we name,
but I think I finally know you again the same.

When I looked over, you were already there,
like you’d been waiting in the passenger air.
